subject: ap-government
theme: Political participation — through voting, parties, interest groups, campaigns, and social movements — connects citizens to government, but structural features of the American system produce systematic inequalities in who participates and whose preferences prevail.
core_concepts: Suffrage expansion: the franchise was progressively extended through the 15th Amendment (race, 1870), 19th Amendment (women, 1920), 24th Amendment (poll tax abolished, 1964), 26th Amendment (age 18, 1971), and the Voting Rights Act of 1965 — each expansion faced political resistance and required sustained political mobilization.

Voter turnout patterns: presidential elections (~55-65%) exceed midterms (~40-50%), which exceed primaries (~15-30%); turnout rises with age, education, and income; formal barriers (registration requirements, ID laws) and psychological factors (low political efficacy, civic disengagement) depress participation among younger, lower-income voters.

Political parties as linkage institutions: parties recruit candidates, organize government, mobilize voters, and translate citizen preferences into policy — the two-party system is reinforced by winner-take-all Electoral College rules, plurality voting in single-member districts, and ballot access laws; third parties face structural barriers despite occasionally receiving significant vote shares (Perot 1992: 18.9% popular vote, 0 electoral votes).

Interest groups and PACs: interest groups advocate for specific constituencies through lobbying, litigation, and PAC contributions; super PACs (post-Citizens United) may raise and spend unlimited amounts on independent expenditures; the free rider problem — individuals can benefit from collective goods without joining or paying — undermines interest group organization for diffuse interests.

Citizens United v. FEC (2010): the Supreme Court struck down restrictions on independent corporate and union expenditures as violations of First Amendment free speech — distinguishing independent spending (unlimited) from direct contributions (still capped); the decision enabled super PACs and dramatically increased outside spending in elections.

Voting behavior models: party identification (strongest predictor — stable, learned from family); retrospective voting (punish incumbents for poor performance); prospective voting (vote for promising future policy); issue voting (single issue drives choice); rational choice theory (maximize personal benefit) — each model explains different voter behavior patterns.

Media and campaigns: media shapes political participation through agenda-setting (what issues citizens consider important), priming (which criteria voters use to evaluate leaders), and framing (how issues are presented shapes interpretation); horse race journalism prioritizes poll standings over policy substance; the shift from broadcast to digital media has fragmented audiences and enabled micro-targeted campaign advertising.

Linkage institutions: parties, elections, interest groups, and media all serve as linkage institutions — connecting citizen preferences to governmental action. When these institutions function well, democratic accountability works; when they are captured by narrow interests or distorted by money and information asymmetries, representative government is undermined.
exam_tip: AP exam questions on Unit 5 frequently use voter turnout data tables, campaign finance scenarios involving Citizens United or super PACs, or party-vs-interest-group distinction questions — know the structural barriers to third-party success (Electoral College, plurality voting, ballot access), the difference between direct contributions and independent expenditures under campaign finance law, and the specific holding and significance of Citizens United.
